title: PCI-DSS 1.3 — CDE network boundary: unauthorised flows between CDE and untrusted networks
exclusions: Does NOT enforce the firewall (that is the NSC); detects allowed flows between CDE and untrusted zones that are not in the Req-1.2.1 approved list.
evidence: Saved search 'pci_1_3_cde_boundary' (every 15m) emits: src, src_zone, dest, dest_zone, dest_port, protocol, approved, to compliance_summary.

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI-DSS 1.3 Network segmentation between CDE and other networks — directly evidenced.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that PCI-DSS 1.3 (CDE network boundary) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.91: PCI-DSS 1.3 — CDE network boundary: unauthorised flows between CDE and untrusted networks.

compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SOC-2 CC6.6 (Encryption in transit) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.91: PCI-DSS 1.3 — CDE network boundary: unauthorised flows between CDE and untrusted networks.

controlTest.positiveScenario: A synthetic allow rule permitting UNTRUSTED→CDE on port 3306 fires within 15m.
controlTest.negativeScenario: A flow approved in pci_approved_flows.csv is absent.

dataSources: `index=netfw` sourcetype IN (pan:traffic,cisco:asa); zone lookup `cde_zones.csv`; approved-flows lookup `pci_approved_flows.csv`.
app: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757) (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=netfw sourcetype IN (pan:traffic,cisco:asa) action=allow earliest=-15m
| lookup cde_zones.csv ip=src OUTPUT zone AS src_zone
| lookup cde_zones.csv ip=dest OUTPUT zone AS dest_zone
| where (src_zone="CDE" AND dest_zone="UNTRUSTED") OR (src_zone="UNTRUSTED" AND dest_zone="CDE")
| lookup pci_approved_flows.csv src_zone dest_zone dest_port protocol OUTPUT approved
| where approved!="yes" OR isnull(approved)
| stats count BY src src_zone dest dest_zone dest_port protocol approved
description: Detects any CDE-to-untrusted or untrusted-to-CDE flow that was allowed by the NSC but is not on the approved-flows list. PCI-DSS 1.3 mandates network segmentation between CDE and other networks; an allowed unapproved flow is a direct non-conformance.
value: Provides QSAs the direct evidence that CDE boundary controls are intact and deviations are captured within 15 minutes.
implementation: (1) Onboard Palo Alto + ASA traffic; (2) maintain cde_zones.csv (CIDR → zone) and pci_approved_flows.csv; (3) schedule UC every 15m; (4) hit opens a P1 to NSC team; (5) daily report to compliance officer. Indexes required: `index=netfw`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cisco:asa`, `sourcetype=pan:traffic`.
visualization: Sankey of src_zone→dest_zone with unapproved highlighted, table of unapproved flows, single value 'unapproved CDE flows (1h)'.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user, All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Change (CIM data model), All_Changes dataset.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `netfw` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `1.3`. The control objective is: "Evidence that PCI-DSS 1.3 (CDE network boundary) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.11.91: PCI-DSS 1.3 — CDE network boundary: unauthorised flows between CDE and untrusted networks." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `netfw` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Onboard Palo Alto + ASA traffic. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** maintain cde_zones.csv (CIDR → zone) and pci_approved_flows.csv. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** schedule UC every 15m. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** hit opens a P1 to NSC team. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 5:** daily report to compliance officer. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last 15 minutes, grouped by `src, src_zone, dest, dest_zone`. For "PCI-DSS 1.3 — CDE network boundary: unauthorised flows between CDE and untrusted networks," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the PCI-DSS control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `src, src_zone, dest, dest_zone` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(src) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Change` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.user, All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Change` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.11.91: PCI-DSS 1.3 — CDE network boundary: unauthorised flows betwe]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -15m
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.11.91,reg=PCI-DSS
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=netfw sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `src`, `dest`, `dest_port`, `protocol`, `action`, `_time`. Run:

```spl
index=netfw sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table src, dest, dest_port, protocol, action, _time
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.11.91: PCI-DSS evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Sankey Of Src Zone→Dest Zone With Unapproved Highlighted (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Table Of Unapproved Flows (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single Value 'Unapproved Cde Flows (1H)' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**PCI-DSS-specific failure modes:**

• Scope creep: PCI Requirement 1.2 requires CDE (Cardholder Data Environment) documentation. Every PCI DSS detection must explicitly reference the CDE inventory lookup; an 'all hosts' search that happens to catch CDE hosts is NOT compliant evidence — the QSA needs to see the scope boundary enforced in the query itself.

• Log retention vs online availability: Req 10.5.1 requires 1 year of audit log retention with at least 3 months immediately available. The index's `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` must be >= 31536000; additionally, Splunk's summary index or an accelerated datamodel must cover the last 90 days for immediate auditor retrieval. One-year hot/warm is not required; the distinction matters for cost.

• Daily log review (Req 10.4): PCI requires DAILY review evidence. Detections backing this control must produce signed evidence per calendar day, not per 24-hour rolling window — a search scheduled at 23:55 daily is compliant; one running every 24h from an arbitrary start time is not.

• FIM (File Integrity Monitoring, Req 11.5.2) scope: FIM detections must cover critical system files on CDE devices, not 'all changes anywhere.' Use the CDE host list as the first-pass filter; flags on non-CDE hosts dilute the signal and are out of scope for the RoC.
grandmaExplanation: We detect any CDE-to-untrusted or untrusted-to-CDE flow that was allowed by the NSC but is not on the approved-flows list. PCI DSS is about protecting card and payment data when we run these checks.
